title: Setup
text:
Below are the instructions to setup you environment in the workshop webVNC and environment.





Copy the directory containing the directories and data you will use for the workshop. The move into the directory you copied.

cp -r /pub14/tea/nsc206/CGR/maaslin3_workshop ~/.
cd ~/maaslin3_workshop







Load the conda/mamba environment maaslin3 with a use script made for this environment.

. usemaaslin3

Instructions on creating the environment for your own future analysis are in the appendix.
A tutorial on jupyter-notebook is on the next page if you need it.

title: Timepoint challenge
text:
As the final page of this relative abundance section you’ll go through the below tasks with accompanying MCQs.
This will involve interrogating the results of association analysis using the TimepointName metadata. It contains one comparison of Poolvisits7,8,9 as the query and Poolvisits1,2,3 as the reference.
Solutions are in the expandable boxes but please make a good effort on attempting the task before viewing them.





You can save then close and halt your “4-Plots.ipynb” notebook.
Create and open a new jupyter notebook called “5-Timepoint.ipynb” to create and run the code for this challenge.
Create a markdown cell at the top with:

# 5-Timepoint
## Libraries & data

In a code cell load the required libraries and data.

#Library
library("maaslin3")
library("IRdisplay")
library("tidyverse")
#Data
load("metagenome_output/significant_results.RData")




As the first task create a tibble called timepoint_sig_res_tbl that contains the significant results for the TimepointName comparisons. In other words extract all the columns from sig_res_tbl where the metadata column equals "TimepointName".
Glimpse the tibble after you have created it.


Timepoint results solution


#Rows of Timepoint results
timepoint_sig_res_tbl <-
    sig_res_tbl |>
    #Filter metadata for TimepointName
    dplyr::filter(metadata=="TimepointName")
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |> dplyr::glimpse()








Filter and view the tibble timepoint_sig_res_tbl so it only displays the abundance model results. Additionally, only view columns 1, 5, 9, 11, 12, and 13.
Then answer the below MCQs. You can create code or view the tibble to answer the questions.


Abundance results tibble


#View table of abundance models
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |>
    #Only have one comparison so don't need value column
    select(c(1,5,9,11,12,13)) |>
    #Abundance models
    filter(model=="abundance")



How many significant abundance associations are there (based on qval_individual)?

 5 6 14




MCQ 1 code solution


#MCQ1
#How many significant abundance assoications are there?
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |>
    #Abundance models
    filter(model=="abundance") |>
    #Filter by qval significance
    filter(qval_individual<0.1) |>
    #Count number of rows
    nrow()



Which feature has the largest positive coefficient (i.e. higher abundance in the second set of visits) whilst also being significant (qval_individual < 0.1)?

 Bifidobacterium vaginale Dietzia maris Stutzerimonas stutzeri




MCQ 2 code solution


#MCQ2
#Which feature has the largest positive coefficient 
#(i.e. higher abundance in the second set of visits)
# whilst also being significant (qval_individual < 0.1)?
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |>
   #Abundance models
   dplyr::filter(model=="abundance") |>
   #Filter by qval significance
   dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1) |>
   #Maximum coefficient
   dplyr::slice_max(coef) |>
   #Pull out feature name
   dplyr::pull(feature)



Which feature has the most negative coefficient (i.e. lower abundance in the second set of visits) whilst also being significant (qval_individual < 0.1)?

 Bifidobacterium vaginale Dietzia maris Stutzerimonas stutzeri




MCQ 3 code solution


#MCQ3
#Which feature has the most negative coefficient 
#(i.e. lower abundance in the second set of visits)
# whilst also being significant (qval_individual < 0.1)?
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |>
   #Abundance models
   dplyr::filter(model=="abundance") |>
   #Filter by qval significance
   dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1) |>
   #Maximum coefficient
   dplyr::slice_min(coef) |>
   #Pull out feature name
   dplyr::pull(feature)


Look at the linear TimepointName plots of Bifidobacterium vaginale, Dietzia maris, and Stutzerimonas stutzeri and answer the below MCQs.

Which feature has the lowest prevalence (i.e. has the fewest number of samples where the feature is present)?

 Bifidobacterium vaginale Dietzia maris Stutzerimonas stutzeri



Which feature has the highest median value for relative abundance in the Poolvisits1,2,3 group?

 Bifidobacterium vaginale Dietzia maris Stutzerimonas stutzeri








Filter and view the tibble timepoint_sig_res_tbl so it only displays the prevalence model results. Additionally, only view columns 1, 5, 9, 11, 12, and 13.
Then answer the below MCQs. You can create code or view the tibble to answer the questions.


Prevalence results tibble


#View table of prevalence models
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |>
    #Only have one comparison so don't need value column
    dplyr::select(c(1,5,9,11,12,13)) |>
    #Prevalence models
    dplyr::filter(model=="prevalence")



How many of the associations have errors?

 1 2 14




MCQ 1 code solution


#MCQ1
#How many associations have errors?
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |>
    #Abundance models
    dplyr::filter(model=="prevalence") |>
    #error filtering
    tidyr::drop_na(error) |>
    nrow()



How many significant associations with positive coefficients are there with no error?

 1 2 14




MCQ 2 code solution


#MCQ2
#How many significant associations with positive coefficients are there with no error?
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |>
    #Abundance models
    dplyr::filter(model=="prevalence") |>
    #Filter by qval individual and coef
    dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1 & coef >0) |>
    #Remove associations with errors
    dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na)) |>
    nrow()



How many significant associations with negative coefficients are there with no error?

 1 2 14




MCQ 3 code solution


#MCQ3
#How many significant associations with negative coefficients are there with no error?
timepoint_sig_res_tbl |>
    #Abundance models
    dplyr::filter(model=="prevalence") |>
    #Filter by qval individual and coef
    dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1 & coef <0) |>
    #Remove associations with errors
    dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na)) |>
    nrow()


Look at the logisitic TimepointName plots of Acinetobacter ursingii, Corynebacterium suicordis, and, Micrococcus luteus and answer the below MCQs.

Which feature has the highest prevalence % for Pool_visits1,2,3?

 Acinetobacter ursingii Corynebacterium suicordis Micrococcus luteus



Which feature has the biggest difference between the 2 prevalence percentages?

 Acinetobacter ursingii Corynebacterium suicordis Micrococcus luteus



Which feature has the highest prevalence (i.e. largest amount of samples where the feature is present)?

 Acinetobacter ursingii Corynebacterium suicordis Micrococcus luteus


Excellent! That is this section complete. I suggest quickly looking over the first page of this section and its summary of the various parts to get a quick recap.

title: Summary interpretation
text:
Summary interpretation
With the plot we’ll interpret the results of Cutibacterium acnes and some overall patterns.





C.acnes
Cutibacterium acnes is the top feature in the plot so lets discuss what the plot can tell us.
The top associations are the abundance associations for cheek and scalp. We can see both their coefficients are higher than the median coefficient via the circles in the coefficient plot. Additionally, these circle are dark purple indicating high significance (q-value < 0.1).
Interestingly the coefficients of the prevalence models are high but not significant (>0.1). This seems strange due to the fact the coefficient values are the largest in the coefficient plot (>10).
To find out why this is we can look in the significant_results.html and search for Cutibacterium acnes. Carry this out and you’ll see four rows, 2 for the cheek and scalp abundance modelling and 2 for their prevalence modelling. The individual q-values for the prevalence modelling are both 0. What’s causing this lack of significance?
In this case it is lack of power. The column N_not_zero shows 170 out of the 172 samples have the feature present. With only 2 samples not containing the feature the prevalence association test does not have enough power to give a significant result.
Why is the coefficient so large then? This can occur in logistics modelling when the feature is present in all the query samples but absent in only a few reference samples. The other way round can cause highly negative coefficient values.
Note: In your own analysis you may need to look at all the results all_results.tsv to find feature associations that are not present in the significant results output.


Overall
Some overall patterns to notice are:

Scalp versus axilla has similar results to cheek versus axilla, axilla was a good reference to choose
The top five features have strong positive coefficients for abundance for the scalp and cheek comparisons
The middle features (G.adiacens to L.mirabilis, except S.capitis) have higher prevalence in the cheek samples compared to axilla
The bottom features (A.octavius to C.pseudokroppenstedtii) are in lower abundance in the scalp and cheek samples compared to the axilla samples
Features where prevalence is more likely with higher read depths also seem to have associations of lower prevalence in the TimepointNamePool_visit7,8,9
There are no significant abundance associations for Reads or TimepointNamePool_visits7,8,9

That’s been a lot but we have even more!

title: Replot
text:
When we ran maaslin3() it created 250 plot files as we set max_pngs = 250. Unfortunately this means that some of the significant associations we want to interrogate do not have plots.
Fortunately rather than rerunning the maaslin3() function, which can take a long time, we can run the function maaslin_plot_results_from_output().
Run the below code to recreate the plots whilst making more by setting max_pngs= to 500.

#MaAsLin3 replot
maaslin3::maaslin_plot_results_from_output(
    output = 'metagenome_output',
    metadata = metadata,
    normalization = 'TSS',
    transform = 'LOG',
    #Max value of qval_individual value for plot to be generated
    #qval_joint has no effect on this
    max_significance = 0.1,
    median_comparison_abundance = TRUE,
    median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE,
    max_pngs = 500
)






Unfortunately when running maaslin_plot_results_from_output() we can’t copy the parameters from our maaslin3() function. But at the same time maaslin_plot_results_from_output() uses some of the same parameters as maaslin3() and we need to have these being identical to our previous maaslin3() function.
Below are the parameters used above:

output: This is the output directory of our maaslin3() function

Our replot function will uses its contents to recreate the plots
The recreated and new plots will be placed in the relevant directories within this directory

metadata: The metadata table to be used for the plot generation

We are using the same table as our maaslin3() function
This can be an opportunity to rename variables and values (see: MaAsLin3 manual on replotting)

normalization: Same as maaslin3()
transform: Same as maaslin3()
max_significance = 0.1: Same as maaslin3()

This indicates the qval_individual value of the features

median_comparison_abundance: Same as maaslin3()
median_comparison_prevalence: Same as maaslin3()
max_pngs: New maximum number of pngs to create

If there are less significant features than the max number set you will get as many plots as significant features

title: Read counts
text:
MaAsLin3 identifies prevalence (presence/absence) associations. Prevalence of organisms can be greatly affected by the read depth of the different samples. When using abundance values (not quantitative abundance values) the read numbers/depth for each sample should be included as a continuous fixed effect.





We have the read counts/depth in two separate files for this dataset. Read these in and check their contents.

#Read in the export files
export_40 <- read.csv("export_samples_2026-04-16_14_40.tsv", sep = "\t")
export_41 <- read.csv("export_samples_2026-04-16_14_41.tsv", sep = "\t")
#Check
export_40 |> dplyr::slice(1:5)
export_41 |> dplyr::slice(1:5)

Thankfully the sample names in the Name variable are the same as in our count table and metadata. Unfortunately, the read counts are set as characters with M, and G representing 1,000,000s and 1,000,000,000s.






Let’s check if there are any other letters used. We’ll use the stringr::sub_str() function in the code.

#Check letters used in read counts
export_40 |>
    #Pull Reads out as a vector
    dplyr::pull(Reads) |>
    #Extract last character
    stringr::str_sub(-1) |>
    #See unique values
    unique()
#Repeat for export_41
export_41 |>
    dplyr::pull(Reads) |>
    stringr::str_sub(-1) |>
    unique()

We can see the letters k, M, and G are used. There are some numbers but these are from read counts with less than 1,000 reads.
In your future analysis I would recommend looking at the entire table to see if it looks all ok before continuing. Of course, if you can get the actual whole numbers of reads that would be ideal (i.e. 3,543,297 rather than 3.543M).






In the next code block we’ll bind these two data.frames, and select the Names and Reads columns. Then we’ll need to change the Reads column to be proper numeric values. We’ll carry this out with the following steps:

Mutate the character to change k to e3, M to e6 etc.

e3 is scientific notation meaning times 10 to the power of 3 (i.e. *1000)

Convert the Reads to numerics

e3 will be converted to a trailing 000, e6 to 000000 etc.
167e3 -> 167000



#Edits
read_counts <- export_40 |>
    #Bind the 2 data.frames by rows (one on top of the other)
    dplyr::bind_rows(export_41) |>
    #Keep only Name and Reads columns
    dplyr::select(Name, Reads) |>
    #Change letters to R exponential notation
    #e.g. 10k becomes 10e3
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=gsub("k","e3",Reads)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=gsub("K","e3",Reads)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=gsub("M","e6",Reads)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=gsub("G","e9",Reads)) |>
    #Convert read numbers to numerics
    dplyr::mutate(Reads=as.numeric(Reads))
#Check
read_counts |> slice(1:5)

Great, that looks all good.






Our final step is to bind the read counts to the metadata table correctly.
First, let’s extract the sample names from the metadata table.

#Extract row names (sample names) from metadata
samples <- row.names(metadata)

With this vector we can remove any samples in the read counts table that are not present in the metadata.

#Remove samples in read_counts not present in metadata
read_counts <- read_counts |>
    dplyr::filter(Name %in% samples)

Next we’ll ensure the order of samples in read_counts is the same as in metadata.

#Match the order of the 2 dfs by the sample names
read_counts <- read_counts[order(match(read_counts$Name, samples)),]
#Check read_counts Name column is identical to row names of metadata
identical(row.names(metadata), read_counts$Name)

If the TRUE statement appears from the identical() function you can add the read counts to metadata. If FALSE appears you will need to do some debugging and fix the issue.

#Add read count column to metadata df
metadata$Reads <- read_counts$Reads
#Check
metadata[1:5,]







We’ll open a new jupyter notebook in the next part. Save your taxa_table and metadata objects as files so you can load them into the next notebook.

save(taxa_table,"taxa_table.RData")
save(metadata,"metadata.RData")

You can now save then close and halt the notebook.
With our count table and we can move onto the MaAsLin3 analysis.

title: Plot types
text:
As a recap the four different types of plots are below.


For abundance associations.


Box plot showing the log base 2 relative abundance values of the features.




Coefficient represents the fold change from the reference to the query group.



Scatter plot of log base 2 relative abundance (y) against continuous effect (x, read depth in below figure) with best fit line.




Coefficient represents the amount of change the variable causes, i.e. the slope.




For prevalence associations.


Tile plot showing the number of samples where the feature is present and absent for each grouping.




The coefficient indicates the difference of log-odds/logit of the feature being present in query samples compared to the reference samples.



Box plot of the continuous value (read depth in below figure) split by samples where the feature is present and absent.




The coefficient represents the amount of increased/decreased likelihood (log-odds/logit) of the feature’s presence with a higher value of the metadata.

title: Notebook setup
text:
Notebook setup



The first step is to ensure you have carried out the setup instructions and run through the jupyter tutorial if you are not familiar with it.
Navigate to your directory “~/maaslin3_workshop/relative” in your jupyter file explorer.
Prior to analysis you’ll need to create a new jupyter notebook called 1-Data.ipynb.
At the top create a markdown cell with the following headers.

# 1-Data
## Libraries

Then we’ll load all the libraries we need in a new code cell.

library("tidyverse")

Please create other useful markdown cells in this and future notebooks. Once you’re ready we’ll start with data importing and processing in the next page.

title: Metadata
text:
As with most analysis we require metadata. Our metadata table requires:

Columns of the different metadata variables

The column names are the names of the variables

Rows of observations per sample

The row names need to contain the same sample names as the count table







The first step is to read in and check our metadata.

#Read in metadata
metadata <- read.csv("Metagenome_metadata.csv", sep=",", row.names=1)
#Check rows 1 to 5
metadata |> dplyr::slice(1:5)







There are three main types of metadata variables for MaAsLin3. These are:

Categorical fixed effects

A categorical variable that is related to your hypothesis and requires evaluation
These need to be factors in your metadata

The first level is the reference group
All other levels are compared to the reference in pairwise fashion (i.e.the query)
For example healthy vs disease states

We will use SampleSite and TimePointName as categorical fixed effects

Continuous fixed effects

This could be a continuous variable that is related to your hypothesis and requires evaluation (e.g. dosage)
Or it could be a continuous control variable (e.g. age)
We will use Reads (read counts/depth) as a continuous control variable (see the read counts page)

Random effects

Categorical variables to include that you are not necessarily interested in evaluating
These don’t need to be factors since a reference group is not required
An example includes experimental blocks, different sequencing runs, or subjects
We will use SubjectID as a random effect


More theory and considerations are included in the later pages:

Fixed effects
Random effects







We are going to use SampleSite and TimePointName as categorical fixed effects. Therefore, we need to convert them to factors.
Before factorisation, let’s see what groupings are in the two variables.

metadata |> dplyr::pull(SampleSite) |> unique()
metadata |> dplyr::pull(TimepointName) |> unique()

We have 3 for SampleSite. Let’s use Axilla as the reference group and Cheek and Scalp as the queries.
We only have 2 for TimePointName and we will use Pool_visits1,2,3 as the reference group since its the first set of visits.
Type and run the below code to factorise the two metadata variables. The code uses some piping and the dplyr::mutate() function.

#Factorise categorical fixed effects
metadata <- metadata |>
    #Factorise SampleSite with Axilla as the reference level
    dplyr::mutate(SampleSite=factor(SampleSite, 
                    levels = c("Axilla", "Cheek", "Scalp"))) |>
    #Factorise TimepointName with the first set of visits as the reference level
    dplyr::mutate(TimepointName=factor(TimepointName, 
                    levels = c("Pool_visits1,2,3","Pool_visits7,8,9")))

Great, next we’ll add read counts/depth to the metadata table.

title: MaAsLin3
text:
For our analysis we will immediately run the maaslin3() function. It takes quite a while to run so we’ll use pre-made results instead of running the code.
Additionally, this part contains a lot of theory on the following:

lme4 specification: The formula containing the fixed and random effects
Fixed effects: The effects your hypothesis are interested in
Random effects: Effects your hypothesis is not necessarily interested in but you want to control for
Median comparison: When to and whn notto use median comparison in association testing






Create and open a new jupyter notebook called “2-MaAsLin3.ipynb”.
Create a markdown cell at the top with:

# 2-MaAsLin3
## Libraries

In a code cell load the required libraries and data.

#Library
library("maaslin3")
library("tidyverse")
#Load data
load("taxa_table.RData")
load("metadata.RData")







Run the MaAsLin3 analysis.
Note: For the workshop please read through the below but don’t actually run the command. Instead you’ll already have the pre-made output directory (metagenome_output) in your directory.

#Set a random seed
#We'll use 1 here but I would recommend a random number between 100 and 1000
set.seed(1)
#MaAsLin3
fit_out <- maaslin3(input_data = taxa_table,
                    input_metadata = metadata,
                    output = 'metagenome_output',
                    formula = '~ SampleSite + TimepointName + Reads + (1|SubjectID)',
                    normalization = 'TSS',
                    transform = 'LOG',
                    augment = TRUE,
                    standardize = TRUE,
                    max_significance = 0.1,
                    median_comparison_abundance = TRUE,
                    median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE,
                    small_random_effects=FALSE,
                    max_pngs = 250,
                    cores = 1)
#Set random seed back to null
set.seed(NULL)







The parameters we have used are described below.

input_data: Our formatted count table containing abundance values of species against samples.
input_metadata: Our formatted metadata table for our samples
output: Name of output directory

This will be created by the function

formula: The comparison formula in lme4 specification

Details in lme4 formula page

normalization: Normalisation of the counts before transformation and analysis. Options include:

'TTS' (default): Total Sum Scaling

Converts the counts to fractional relative abundance values so total count of each sample = 1

'CLR': Centred Log Ratio
'NONE': No normalisation

transform: Transformation of feature counts after normalisation but before analysis

Allows MaAsLin3 and us to see the log difference of features between comparisons/groups
Options include:

LOG: Base 2 log transformation (recommended)

Our TSS normalised values will all become -ve values or 0 (log(1, base=2) = 0, log(0.1, base=2)=-3.321928)

PLOG: Pseudo-log with a pseudo-count of half the dataset’s minimum non-zero abundance replacing zeroes

Suggested for metabolomics data

NONE


augment: Default is TRUE

Avoids linear separability and is almost always recommended

standardise: When TRUE it applies z-scores to continuous metadata variables so they are on the same scale

The default is TRUE and is recommended
Allows comparison of coefficients across metadata variables

max_significance: FDR corrected q-value threshold for significance

This will determine what associations are written as signifiant and what plots are created
Associations with an individual or joint q-value less than the threshold will be included in the significant results out
Only associations with an individual q-value lower than the threshold will be plotted

median_comparison_abundance & median_comparison_prevalence: Whether to compare coefficients against median coefficient (TRUE) or against 0

For normal taxa abundance data set median_comparison_abundance = TRUE and median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE
More info, including when to use TRUE and FALSE, in Median comparison page

small_random_effect: Replace random effects with fixed effects in logistic prevalence model

default FALSE
Use TRUE to handle low number of observations per group
More information in the Random effect page


Full information here: MaAslin3 analysis options

title: Significant results table
text:
There are various output files in our output directory metagenome. The first we will look at is the text file significant_results.tsv. This contains all the significant features maaslin3() found.
This significance is based on the individual q-value or the joint q-value. Additionally the rows are ordered from most to least significant (i.e. ascending order based on the qval_individual column).





Save then close and halt your “2-MaAsLin3.ipynb” notebook.
Create and open a new jupyter notebook called “3-Text_output.ipynb”.
Create a markdown cell at the top with:

# 3-Text output
## Libraries

In a code cell load the required libraries.

#Library
library("DT")
library("tidyverse")







To view the significant results we need to first read in the file. In this case we’ll read it in as a tibble with readr::read_delim() and then glimpse it.

sig_res_tbl <- readr::read_delim(
    file = "./metagenome_output/significant_results.tsv", 
    delim="\t")
sig_res_tbl |> glimpse()

There are many columns/variables which are explained in the next page: Metrics.






To load the significant results easily in other notebooks we’ll save the sig_res_tbl object as a file.

#Save significant results table
save(sig_res_tbl, file="./metagenome_output/significant_results.RData")







You can view the whole table in R:

sig_res_tbl

With this object you can carry out various commands to pull out specific data.
Optionally, you can open the file (significant_results.tsv) by navigating to the metagenome directory through your jupyter file explorer. Once in the directory you can click on the file to open it.
Neither of these methods are convenient. In your own future analysis you can use excel but we’ll show you another option below.






We can create a html table with the package DT and its function DT::datatable(). In the function we specify the data.frame/tibble to convert to HTML. Additionally, we specify options = list(pageLength = 20) so the resulting html will show 20 rows at a time.
DT package homepage

#Convert table to html
sig_res_dt <- DT::datatable(sig_res_tbl, options = list(pageLength = 20))

The above code created an R object which we then need to save as an HTML file. We’ll carry this out with htmlwidgets::saveWidget(). The option selfcontained = must be set to TRUE for this to function correctly.
htmlwidgets::savewdigets() code documentation

#Save html
htmlwidgets::saveWidget(
    #Input widget/html
    widget = sig_res_dt,
    #Output html
    file = "./metagenome_output/significant_results.html",
    #Save as a single self contained HTML
    selfcontained = TRUE)







With the HTML created navigate into the metagenome directory via the jupyter file browser. Click on your HTML file (significant_results.html) to open it.
The html is interactive with the following features:

Order the table by a specific column by clicking on its column names

Click on it once for descending order
Click on it twice for ascending order

Set the number of rows to display with the drop down on the top left (“Show X entries”)

Choices are: 10, 20, 25, 50, and 100

Search for specific text/numbers with the search bar on the top right

Useful if you are interested in the results of a specific species or genus

Navigate across the pages with the arrows and page numbers on the bottom right
View the total number of rows (i.e. significant features) on the bottom left

title: Fixed effects
text:
Fixed effects are any term related to your hypothesis that requires evaluation and any continuous control variables.
We are interested in the differences of our three body sites (Axilla, Cheek and Scalp) so we are used it as a fixed effect. We are also interested in the difference from the first set of pool visits to the next set so we used TimepointName as a fixed effect.
To control for the read counts/depths of the samples we will use Reads as a fixed effect. This uses it as a continuous control variable.


When using fixed effects you can look at the main effect, interactions, or both of multiple variables.
The main effects is when looking at the effect of one variable by itself. Interactions are when you combine variables.
In our formula we specified the formula to only look at main effects. We will look at interactions later.



Below is a list of examples on how to specify the usage of fixed effects.

a: Main effect of a
a + b: Main effect of a and b (no interaction)
a:b: Interaction of a and b (no main effects)
a * b: Main effects and interaction of a and b (expands to a + b + a:b)
(a+b+c)^2: Main effects and 2-way interactions (no 3-way interaction) of a, b , and c (expands to a + b + c + a:b + a:c + b:c)
(a+b)*c: all main effects and pairwise interactions between a and c, and b and c (not between a and b) (expands to a + b + c + a:c + b:c)
0 + a: 0 suppresses the intercept resulting in a model that has one parameter per level of a (identical to : a - 1)

title: Continuous logistic boxplots
text:
Box plots are used to visualise prevalence/logistic modelling associations with continuous effects. These show the range of read sizes for samples where the feature is absent and samples where the feature is absent.
We will use the Reads grouping to look at continuous logistic results.
In this page we will:

Create a significant results table with only prevalence model and read rows
Explain the coefficient value
View and display box plots






Create a significant result tibble only containing Reads associations with the prevalence model.

#Create a tbl with our sig results of interest
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl <- sig_res_tbl |> 
    #Filter to retain metadata Reads rows
    dplyr::filter(metadata == "Reads") |>
    #Filter to retain prevalence model rows
    dplyr::filter(model == "prevalence")
#Glimpse the tibble
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |> dplyr::glimpse()

Extract the top 5 associations by their q-values (qval_individual).

#Top 5 significant associations for prevalence modelling of Reads
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_individual values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,12,14,15))

In this case we actually get 6 rows. This is because the q-value of the 5th and 6th row are identical (i.e. they are both 5th equal place).
The results aren’t terribly significant with fairly low coefficients (<2).






Let’s look at the top association:

Feature: Malassezia globosa
Coefficient: 1.300098
Q-value: 0.002123845

For continuous metadata the coefficient represents the amount of increased/decreased likelihood of the feature’s presence with a higher value of the metadata. A positive value for Reads would indicate the feature is more likely to be present with samples of higher read counts. In other words a positive coefficient means the logit of the feature being present increases.






Logistic continuous plots are horizontal box plots of read counts separated into 2 groupings:

Samples where the feature is absent
Samples where the feature is present

Let’s look at an example.


Below is the box plot created for the logistic modelling of Malassezia globosa for the reads effect.




In this case the plot shows:

Samples where the feature are present tend to have higher read counts
There are more samples where the feature is present (N (not zero) = 145) compared to where it is absent (172-145=27).








View the most significant associations with negative coefficients.

#Top 5 significant associations with negative coefficients
# for prevalence modelling of Reads
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
    #Retain rows with a coefficient value less than 0
    dplyr::filter(coef < 0) |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_individual values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,11,12,14,15))

There only is one association and it has the error: “Prevalence association possibly induced by stronger abundance association”. Additionally, its individual q-value is >0.1 meaning there will not be a plot of it. It was included in the significant results table as its joint q-value is < 0.1.
In fact the association is of our old friend Pseudomonas_E lactis. Its linear prevalence association was very strong for the continuous linear analysis and plots. Therefore, its very likely the above error is true and the prevalence association is due to the abundance association.
It makes sense that you are more likely to get positive coefficient values for reads. The more reads you have the more likely you are to detect all organisms present.






Attempt the below MCQs based on the prevalence modelling with the Reads fixed effect:
Answer the two below MCQs based on the top 6 significant features of Reads with the prevalence model.

Which feature association has the largest coefficient value?

 Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris Cutibacterium granulosum Staphylococcus haemolyticus



Which feature has the highest amount of samples where the feature is absent (i.e. lowest prevalence)?

 Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris Cutibacterium granulosum Staphylococcus haemolyticus


Open and view the reads logistic plots for the following features to answer the next 4 MCQs.

Malassezia globosa
Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E
Staphylococcus capitis
Xanthomonas campestris

Tip: The reads/x-axis scale is identical for all the plots making for easier comparisons.

Which feature has the smallest difference between its median values for the absent and present groupings?

 Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris



Which feature has the highest amount of outliers for the Present box (i.e. points outside the range of the box and whiskers)?

 Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris



Which feature has the highest median value for the absent samples?

 Malassezia globosa Corynebacterium aurimucosum_E Staphylococcus capitis Xanthomonas campestris


Using the object sig_res_reads_prev_tbl, create some code to answer the below questions.

How many associations have a qval_individual value of less than 0.1?

 12 26 172



How many associations are there where the feature is absent in more than 50% of the samples?

 12 26 172


Tip: You can carry out mathematical operations on different columns in a dplyr::filter() function and carry out a comparison/filter on its result.


Code solution


#MCQs
## Significant results with individual q-value < 0.1
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
   filter(qval_individual<0.1) |>
   nrow()
## Number of results where the feature is absent in more than
# 50% of the samples
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
   filter(N_not_zero/N < 0.5) |>
   nrow()

title: Continuous logistic boxplots
text:
Logistic continuous plots are horizontal box plots of read counts separated into 2 groupings:

Samples where the feature is absent
Samples where the feature is present

Let’s look at an example.


Below is the box plot created for the logistic modelling of Malassezia globosa for the reads effect.




In this case the plot shows:

Samples where the feature are present tend to have higher read counts
There are more samples where the feature is present (N (not zero) = 145) compared to where it is absent (172-145=27).

title: Continuous logistic boxplots
text:
View the most significant associations with negative coefficients.

#Top 5 significant associations with negative coefficients
# for prevalence modelling of Reads
sig_res_reads_prev_tbl |>
    #Retain rows with a coefficient value less than 0
    dplyr::filter(coef < 0) |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_individual values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,11,12,14,15))

There only is one association and it has the error: “Prevalence association possibly induced by stronger abundance association”. Additionally, its individual q-value is >0.1 meaning there will not be a plot of it. It was included in the significant results table as its joint q-value is < 0.1.
In fact the association is of our old friend Pseudomonas_E lactis. Its linear prevalence association was very strong for the continuous linear analysis and plots. Therefore, its very likely the above error is true and the prevalence association is due to the abundance association.
It makes sense that you are more likely to get positive coefficient values for reads. The more reads you have the more likely you are to detect all organisms present.

title: Coefficient plot
text:
The coefficient plots display information on the abundance and prevalence models. Below is the coefficient plot by itself (made by manual editing of the summary plot).




Lets go through its features/aesthetics one by one.


The features outside of the plotting area are:

x-axis: The coefficient value of the association
y-axis: The feature (e.g. Cutibacterium acnes)
Facet header: The Fixed effect comparison (e.g. SampleSite Scalp indicates the Scalp against Axilla comparison)







The features inside the plotting area can be split by Abundance and Prevalence modelling.
Lets look at the abundance modelling first.

Circles: The abundance coefficient

x-axis: The position represents its coefficient value
colour: Represents its q-value (PFDR)

Gradient goes from white to dark purple
Gradient range is displayed on the legend (1 -> 1e-31)


Horizontal line: Represents the null hypothesis

In this case this is the median coefficient value of the metadata
We set the abundance median comparison to TRUE, more info: Median comparisons









Triangle: The prevalence coefficient

x-axis: The position represents its coefficient value
colour: Represents its q-value (PFDR)

Gradient goes from white to dark cyan
Gradient range is displayed on the legend (1 -> 1e-31)


Horizontal dashed line: Represents the null hypothesis

In this case this is 0
We set the abundance median comparison to FALSE, more info: Median comparisons








Using the coefficient heatmap, answer the below MCQs.

title: Categorical logistic grid plots
text:
Grid/tile plots are used to visualise prevalence/logistic modelling associations with categorical effects. These show the number of samples where the feature is present and absent in for each group.
We will use the SampleSite grouping to look at categorical logistic results.
In this page we will:

Create a significant results table with only prevalence model and sample site rows
Explain the coefficient value
View and display grid plots

The concept of log-odds is very important in this and the next page. It was covered earlier in this website: logistic modelling





Create a significant result tibble containing SampleSite associations with the prevalence model.

#Create a tbl with our sig results of interest
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl <- sig_res_tbl |> 
    #Filter to retain metadata SampleSite rows
    dplyr::filter(metadata == "SampleSite") |>
    #Filter to retain prevalence model rows
    dplyr::filter(model == "prevalence")

Extract the top 5 associations by their q-values (qval_individual).

#Top 5 significnat associations for prevalence modelling of SampleSite
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_individual values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,12))

In this table all the associations have very low q-values and reasonably high positive coefficients.






The coefficient indicates the difference of log-odds/logit of the feature being present in query samples compared to the reference samples.
The feature in the 1st row is:

feature: Streptococcus infantis_H
name: SampleSiteCheek
coefficient: 3.230279

This indicates that the log-odds/logit of Streptococcus infantis_H being present in Cheek samples compared to Axilla samples is higher by 3.23. In other words the probability of Streptococcus infantis_H being present is higher in Cheek compared to Axilla. This could work if the log-odds of Cheek and Axilla were both negative, both positive, or if Cheek’s log-odds is positive whilst Axilla’s is negative. To know this we need to look at the plot.






Logistic categorical plots shows us how many samples the feature is present and absent in for the different groupings. We’ll look at 2 examples.


Below is the grid/tile plot created for the logistic modelling of Streptococcus infantis_H for the sample site groupings.




In this case the plot shows:

Streptococcus infantis_H is present in 49 out of the 60 cheek samples (82%)

The feature is more likely than not (>50% chance) to be present in cheek samples

Streptococcus infantis_H is present in 7 out of the 52 axilla samples (13%)

The feature is not more likely than not (<50% chance) to be present in axilla samples


The coefficient is positive (3.2) showing the log-odds of the feature being present in cheek samples is higher than the log-odds of the feature being present in axilla samples. This would be an instance of a positive log-odds for cheek (probability > 0.5) and a negative one (probability < 0.5) for Axilla.



Lets look at a different example. Below is the grid plot for Abiotrophia sp001815865, which we’ll refer to as Abiotrophia from now on.




The cheek against axilla association of this feature has a higher positive coefficient value (3.5) than the S.infantis cheek association above (3.2). However, we can see that the log-odds for all three groupings (axilla, cheek, and scalp) would be negative (probability less than 50%).
In this case even though the log-odds is negative for cheek it is still much closer to 1 (100% probability) compared to the axilla sample. This is a reminder that the coefficient is the difference of the log-odds, not the log-odds value of the query group.







As a final example look at the strongest association (by q-value) with negative coefficient values. These associations indicate the feature is less likely to be present in query samples compared to reference samples. The further the value is from 0 the stronger the association.

#Top 5 significant associations with negative coefficients
# for prevalence modelling of SampleSite
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Retain rows with a coefficient value less than 0
    dplyr::filter(coef < 0) |>
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_individual values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,12))

Interesting, we now have some error messages. In this case they state “Prevalence association possibly induced by stronger abundance association”.


Below is the logistic grid plot for Cutibacterium avidum, one of the associations with the error. From the plot we can see that the percentage of cheek samples with presence of the feature is much lower than for axilla samples (18% vs 52%).




To discover the meaning of the error it is best to view the abundance/linear modelling plot for the feature. In the below plot we can see a strong association for the feature when comparing scalp to axilla. Additionally, the abundance of the feature in the scalp samples it is found in are very low. The values are all less than -10 (>0.001 relative abundance) with a median of around -16 (>0.0001 relative abundance).




Additionally, lets look at the results of all the Cutibacterium avidum associations.

#All results of Cutibacterium avidum
sig_res_tbl |>
    #Filter to only retain Cutibacterium avidum associations
    dplyr::filter(feature=="Cutibacterium avidum") |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,8,12))

Here we can see the abundance modelling produces much stronger significance (lower q-values) and have large negative coefficient values. Therefore, MaAsLin3 is unsure if the prevalence association is true biology or if true presence was undetected in samples. A low abundance feature may not be detected due to the inherent nature of upstream wet lab procedures and/or bioinformatics processes.







Attempt the below MCQs based on the prevalence modelling with the SampleSite fixed effect:

Looking at the five most significant associations with negative coefficients, which feature did not have an error?

 Amaricoccus sp. hMGS.04904 Cutibacterium avidum Veillonella parvula_A



Looking at the five most significant associations with positive coefficients, which feature had the highest coefficient?

 Amaricoccus sp. hMGS.04904 Cutibacterium avidum Veillonella parvula_A


Looking at specific tile plots will allow you answer the next 2 questions.

What percentage of cheek samples is Gemella morbillorum present in?

 6% 42% 70%



What percentage of axilla samples is Dialister invisus present in?

 6% 42% 70%


Creating some filtering code will help answer the below questions.
Tip: The below filter function will retain rows where error is NA. Normally filtering won’t work with NA values.

#Retain rows where error is NA
#needs to be part piped code
dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na))


How many significant (based on qval_individual <0.1) scalp associations are there with no errors?

 4 143 215



How many significant (based on qval_individual <0.1) cheek associations are there with no errors?

 4 143 215




Code solution


#MCQs
##Significant scalp associations with no errors
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Filter by q value
    dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1) |> 
    #Filter to retain Scalp associations
    dplyr::filter(name=="SampleSiteScalp") |>
    #Filter to retain rows where error is NA
    dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na)) |>
    #Count rows
    nrow()
##Significant cheek associations with no errors
sig_res_samplesite_prev_tbl |>
    #Filter by q value
    dplyr::filter(qval_individual<0.1) |> 
    #Filter to retain Scalp associations
    dplyr::filter(name=="SampleSiteCheek") |>
    #Filter to retain rows where error is NA
    dplyr::filter(if_any(error, is.na)) |>
    #Count rows
    nrow()

title: Visit investigation
text:
In this page you’ll investigate the associations for the various visit comparisons. As a reminder each visit comparison is against the baseline (visit 1).


The significant associations of the visit comparisons may be masked by the Sample site associations in our summary plot. Therefore, we’ll create a summary plot based on the Visit associations only.
The MaAsLin3 replot function only needs the “all_results.tsv” to work. To produce our desired summary plot we’ll carry out the below steps:

Create a subdirectory to contain our new results and plot
Create an “all_results.tsv” with our visit associations
Replot to only create our new summary plot



Create a subdirectory within “PMP_output” called “Visit_summary”:

dir.create("./PMP_output/Visit_summary")




The replotting function requires the “all_results.tsv”. We’ll create on in the created directory only containing the Visit associations.

#Read in all results
all_res_tbl <- readr::read_delim(
    file = "./PMP_output/all_results.tsv", 
    delim="\t")
#Subset it for only visits
visit_all_tbl <- all_res_tbl |>
    dplyr::filter(metadata=="Visit") |>
    #Arrange by individual q-value
    dplyr::arrange(qval_individual)
#Write out into ./PMP_output/Visit_summary
visit_all_tbl |>
    readr::write_delim("./PMP_output/Visit_summary/all_results.tsv", delim="\t")




Before carrying out the replot function we’ll need to set the order of the coefficient and heatmap variables we want to include.

#Set order of Coefficient plot variables
coef_plot_variables <- c("Visit V2", "Visit V12")
#Set order of heatmap variables for plot recreation
#Do not include V1 since it is the reference
heatmap_variables <-
    c("Visit V2","Visit V3","Visit V4","Visit V5","Visit V6",
     "Visit V7","Visit V8","Visit V9","Visit V10","Visit V11","Visit V12")

Now replot. This time we’ll set plot_association=FALSE so it does not create all the individual plots and we only get the summary plot. This is because all the association/individual plots are already in “PMP_output/figures”.

#MaAsLin3 replot
maaslin3::maaslin_plot_results_from_output(
    output = 'PMP_output/Visit_summary',
    metadata = metadata,
    normalization = 'NONE',
    transform = 'LOG',
    #Max value of qval_individual value for plot to be generated
    #qval_joint has no effect on this
    max_significance = 0.1,
    median_comparison_abundance = FALSE,
    median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE,
    #Heatmap and coefficient variables
    heatmap_vars = heatmap_variables,
    coef_plot_vars = coef_plot_variables,
    #Only plot the summary plot
    plot_association=FALSE,
    max_pngs = 500
)

Open the file through your jupyter file explorer and you’ll hopefully see the below plot.











In the summary plot we can see that the following features have multiple significant features. Below are MCQs based on the association figures for the features.





Look at the logistic/prevalence plots for the following features:

Staphyloccocus hominis
Acinetobacter baumannii
Staphyloccocus aureus

With the plots answer the below MCQs.
Which feature has a significant association for the V4 comparison?

 Staphyloccocus hominis Acinetobacter baumannii Staphyloccocus aureus

Which feature has the lowest prevalence for V1?

 Staphyloccocus hominis Acinetobacter baumannii Staphyloccocus aureus

Which feature has the largest prevalence % difference between V1 and V12?

 Staphyloccocus hominis Acinetobacter baumannii Staphyloccocus aureus







Look at the linear/abundance plots for the following features:

Stretococcus sanguinis
Staphyloccocus epidermidis
Staphyloccocus warneri

Which feature has the lowest media abundance values?

 Stretococcus sanguinis Staphyloccocus epidermidis Staphyloccocus warneri

Which feature has the largest range of abundance values?

 Stretococcus sanguinis Staphyloccocus epidermidis Staphyloccocus warneri

Which feature has the most power (i.e. the most amount samples with the feature present)?

 Stretococcus sanguinis Staphyloccocus epidermidis Staphyloccocus warneri

Superb! Next we’ll look at a variety of different ways to analyse the data with MaAsLin3.
Reminder: Please save your notebooks frequently as you work on them.

title: Quantitative MaAsLin3
text:
For our first set of analysis we will keep it fairly straightforward so we can focus on:

The parameters needed for abundance counts (specifically for the PMP data)
How to utilise longitudinal metadata






Create and open a new jupyter notebook called “2-Visit_and_sample_main_effects.ipynb”.
Create a markdown cell at the top with:

# Visit & Sample main effects
## Libraries

In a code cell load the required libraries and data.

#Library
library("maaslin3")
library("tidyverse")
#Load data
load("taxa_table.RData")
load("metadata.RData")







Run the MaAsLin3 analysis. As the dataset contains a lot fewer species the analysis should not take too long to run (~10 minutes).

#MaAsLin3 analysis
#Set seed so any reruns produce identical results
set.seed(111)
#Command
fit_out <- maaslin3(input_data = taxa_table,
                    input_metadata = metadata,
                    output = 'PMP_output',
                    #Fixed effect
                    #Main effects of Visit and Sample
                    #Random effect of Subject
                    #Does not work with Batch as formula becomes too complex
                    formula = '~ Visit + Sample + (1|Subject)',
                    #No normalisation
                    normalization = 'NONE',
                    transform = 'LOG',
                    augment = TRUE,
                    standardize = TRUE,
                    max_significance = 0.1,
                    #No median comparison as using quantitative abundance
                    median_comparison_abundance = FALSE,
                    median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE,
                    small_random_effects=FALSE,
                    max_pngs = 250,
                    cores = 1)
#Set random seed back to null
set.seed(NULL)




The changes for quantitative abundance analysis are:

normalization = NONE: We do not carry out normalisation as we are interested in the absolute/quantitative abundances not the relative abundances

Source: https://forum.biobakery.org/t/maaslin3-with-estimated-microbial-load/7668

median_comparison_abundance = FALSE: This is always set with absolute/quantitative abundance

Source: https://github.com/biobakery/biobakery/wiki/MaAsLin3#41-absolute-abundance


Analysis is different again if you are using:

Spike-ins: Having a species of known quantitative abundance to scale the other species to
Total abundance scaling: Having a total quantitative abundance of all the species to scale them all to







Parameters are listed in the relative abundance MaAsLin3 page

title: Logistic model
text:
MaAsLin3 carries out binary logistic regression for prevalence (absence/presence) testing.





Binary logistic regression is used to determine the probability of one of two outcomes. In MaAsLin3 this is to test prevalence, determining the probability of a feature to be present given an effect.
With this test we could attempt to answer the following example questions:

Is Helicobacter pylori more likely to be present in patients with ulcers compared to healthy controls?
Are Corynebacterium species more likely to be present in the Axilla compared to the cheek or scalp?
Are certain species more likely to be present/detected in samples with higher sampling depth?

We won’t go into the workings of binary logistics rather we’ll explain its main value: log-odds/logit.






Log-odds, also know as the logit, is equal to the logarithm of the odds. The equation is:
\[
logit = ln(p/1-p)
\]
where:

\(p\) = Probability
\(ln\) = log



For example, lets look at the probability of having the surname Murphy if you lived in Ireland in 2014.

Probability = 0.0164 (i.e 1.64%)
Odds = 0.1752
Logit = -4.044334

For a comparison, lets look at the probability of having the surname Gemmell if you lived in Scotland in 2014.

Probability = 0.0000446 (i.e 0.00446%)
Odds = 0.0000446
Logit = -10.01773

In the above case we can see that the logit has a lower negative value for Gemmell which had a lower probability than Murphy. BUt what is the scale of these values?



The logit scale goes from negative infinity (probability = 0) to positive infinity (probability = 1). Our anchor point is a probability of 50 with a logit value of 0.
Therefroe, a positive logit value means the event is more likely than not to occur (>0.5 probability). Whilst, a negative logit value means the event is more likely to not occur (<0.5 probability).
Some useful benchmark figures are:

Lets see the equation in the form of a plot:









In the plot we can see the logit value slowly changes moving away from a probability of 0.5. Once the probability is >0.99/<0.01 the logit value quickly increases/decreases getting closer and closer to infinity. This is the main reason we use logit scale. It is hard for us to see the difference between a probability of 0.000859 and 0.00006041 but easier for us to differentiate between their logit values of -7.058882 and -9.71429.
I hope this gives you a clear idea of the log-odds/logit value so you interrupt them in future analysis.
More information on how to interpret the logit value is found later in the abundance section: MaAsLin3 coefficients

title: Background
text:
Background



MaAsLin3 is a tool to detect feature associations based on metadata groupings using mixed effect models.
It carries out 2 types of association tests:

Abundance testing

Determines the difference in the abundance values between groups
Carried out with linear modelling

Prevalence testing

Determines the likelihood of features being present based on groupings
Carried out with logistic modelling


Both tests produce q-values plus a joint q-value is given.
Good resource: https://mspeekenbrink.github.io/sdam-r-companion/linear-mixed-effects-models.html#formulating-and-estimating-linear-mixed-effects-models-with-lme4

title: MaAsLin3 resources
text:
MaAsLin3 resources
Below are various resources for MaAsLin3:

MaAsLin3 github main page
MaAsLin3 Tutorial

Interactions: Seeing interactions or 2 effects

Note: It may be easier to create a metadata column combining 2 categorical metadata columns rather than using interactions

Level contrasts: Comparison of ordered effects

This will compare second level against the first, third level against the second etc.
No random effects can be used in the formula when using and ordered effect

Group-wise differences: Produce one overall p/q-value for the variable

Uses an ANOVA or ANOVA-like procedure

title: Linear model
text:
MaAsLin3 carries out linear modelling for abundance testing.


A simple linear regression can be carried two variables. These variables are:

The independent variable: Not dependent on any other variable

Preferably controlled by the experimenter

The dependant variable: Dependant on the independent variable

The below linear regression equation then allows you to predict the value of y when you have the value of x or vice verse.
\[
y = mx + c
\]
Where:

\(y\) = Y variable (dependant variable)
\(m\) = slope coefficient
\(x\) = X variable (independent variable)
\(c\) = intercept coefficient (i.e. y position when x = 0)

title: Mixed effect model
text:
In real life we will commonly have more than two variables in our experiments. Thankfully, linear regression can have three or more variables.
Once you start using more variables they can start to interact with each other in various ways. This is when Mixed Effect Modelling, also known as Hierarchical modelling, can be useful. This is important for us to know as MaAsLin3 stands for Microbiome Multivariable Associations with Linear Models and uses Mixed effect linear models.
In simplest term a mixed effect model is when you have multiple linear regressions for your data based on different nested groups. To explain this further we’ll go through the following concepts:

Nested data
Random intercept
Random slope
Random slope + intercept



Nested data is when your observation are within groups of data. This could be:

Age groups
Demographics
Gender
Experimental blocks
Sequencing runs

Let’s look at a concrete example with our E.coli data from the last page. However, in this case we have more datapoints. Our original data was 75 patients with E.coli that had no detected AMRs. Our new set of 75 patients had E.coli with AMRs. These two sets of patients are our two nested groups.
Note: You can have a lot more than just 2 nested groups.









In this case we can see that our simple linear model based only on E.coli abundance and Antibiotic dosage is not fit for purpose.



The first addition to our linear model to incorporate mixed effects is to add random intercepts. In essence this is allowing each group to have different intercept values.
Let’s see that in practice with our dataset.









The linear models for each nested group now start in good places since they have different intercepts. However, they both have the same slope which does not fit either group very well.



Instead of random intercepts lets use random slopes. This gives each group its own slope.
Below is our data with random slopes but the same intercept.









This looks better but its still not perfect.



In mixed effect models both random slopes and random intercepts are used. This allows you to best account for the differences between the groups.
Below is our final plot with random slopes and random intercepts.









Nice explanation: https://mfviz.com/hierarchical-models/

text:
The count table for MaAsLin3 needs to be a data.frame with:

Columns names of features

In our case species

Row names of sample names

In our case abundance values







First we will import our count data. ALthough the file suffix is “.csv” the file is actually space separated (sep=" ").

#Read in space delimited count data
taxa_table <- read.csv("PMP_counts.csv", sep = " ", row.names=1, check.names=FALSE)
#Check
str(taxa_table)
taxa_table[1:5,1:10]







Our data is in the correct orientation. The only thing we need to do is to converts NAs to 0s.

#Replace NAs with 0
taxa_table[is.na(taxa_table)] <- 0
#View subset
taxa_table[1:5,1:10]

title: Metadata
text:
As with most analysis we require metadata. Our metadata table requires:

Columns of the different metadata variables

The column names are the names of the variables

Rows of observations per sample

The row names need to contain the same sample names as the count table







The first step is to read in and check our metadata.

#Read in metadata
#Also space delimted
metadata <- read.csv("PMP_metadata.csv", sep=" ", row.names=1, check.names=FALSE)
#Check top 5 rows
metadata |> dplyr::slice(1:5)







For our fixed effects we will need to factorise them so we can set the reference level. In our analyses we will only use the following columns for fixed effects:

Visit

Visit, Timepoint, and Month contain the same exact information but with different values
Visit was chosen for clarity and consistency with other metadata variables

Sample

First we’ll need to check the unique values of the columns/variables.

#Unique values of fixed effect variables
metadata |> dplyr::pull(Visit) |> unique()
metadata |> dplyr::pull(Sample) |> unique()

We’ll factorise our fixed effects. For Sample we will use the Axilla group as the reference (just as we did in the relative abundance analysis). For Visit we’ll use V1 as the reference since it is the first time point in this longitudinal data.

#Factorise fixed effects
metadata <- metadata |>
    #Factorise Visit
    dplyr::mutate(
        Visit=factor(Visit, 
            levels = c("V1","V2","V3","V4","V5","V6","V7","V8","V9","V10","V11","V12"))) |>
    #Factorise Sample
    dplyr::mutate(Sample=factor(Sample, levels = c("Axilla", "Cheek", "Scalp")))

Glimpse the metadata table.

metadata |> dplyr::glimpse()







The two variables we will use as random effects are:

Subject
Batch

For clarity/tidiness we’ll change the Batch values from numbers (x) to B then numbers (Bx).

#Change Batch values from x to Bx
#e.g. 1 to B1
metadata <- metadata |>
    mutate(Batch=gsub(pattern = "^", replacement="B", Batch))
#Check top 5 rows
metadata |> dplyr::slice(1:5)

With this both our Batch and Subject variables are character column. This is what we want as we cannot use continuous variables (numbers) as random effects.
View the different Batch and Subject values.

#Unique values of random effects
metadata |> pull(Batch) |> unique()
metadata |> pull(Subject) |> unique()

We have 6 Batches and 20 different Subjects.






We went over the concepts and considerations of small random effects in the relative abundance section. Therefore, let’s check if we should change any parameters for our MaAsLin3 analyses when using our random effects.

#Average group size of random effects
#Batch
metadata |>
    dplyr::count(Batch) |>
    dplyr::pull(n) |> mean()
#Subject
metadata |>
    dplyr::count(Subject) |>
    dplyr::pull(n) |> mean()

Both Batch and Subject have an average number of observations > 4. Therefore there is no issue with small rand effects.






As we are directly using quantitative abundance counts we do not need to use reads. Therefore we can move onto analysis.
Save the counts and metadata objects as files.

save(taxa_table, file = "taxa_table.RData")
save(metadata, file = "metadata.RData")

Then:

Save your jupyter notebook
Close and halt your jupyter-notebook

title: Quantitative
text:
In this section of the course you’ll be introduced to analysing quantitative abundance data with MaAsLin3. This will involve a variety of different ways to analyse the data.





Our dataset was produced with Precision Microbiome Profiling which produces quantitative abundance counts for a set of microbial species. Our data contains counts for 52 species and a column for 16s rRNA.
The 16s rRNA column is an assay of its own that quantifies all bacteria. Its number will not equal the total of the other other columns.
The metadata for the dataset includes:

Subject: The individual
Visit: The visit number corresponding to month (V1-V12).

Starts from October and ends the following September
This serves as longitudinal data

Sample: The sample site: Axilla, Cheek, or Scalp
Batch: The Batch number





Notebook setup: Setup of the quantitative directory and the first jupyter notebook
Data: Reading in data and formatting it for MaAsLin3
Quantitative analysis: Running and interpretation of maaslin3() with the quantitative and longitudinal data

title: Summary plot
text:
With any MaAsLin3 analysis the best output to view first is the summary plot.


You can view your summary plot in the jupyter file explorer or you can view it below on this page.




There is a lot of useful information but annoyingly the Visits are not in chronilogical order. This is not ideal for our time points. Let’s fix that.






We can use the function maaslin3::maaslin_plot_results_from_output() to recreate our plots. Importantly we can use the parameters coef_plot_vars= and heatmap_vars= to set which variables will be included in the heatmap and coefficient plot. Additionally, it sets the order of the variables too.
The easiest option is to create vectors of the variable names. Some important notes:

The variables names need to be exactly as they are shown in the summary plot

For example the first variable in the plot above is “Sample Scalp” i.e. The metadata name, a space, and then the value

You can add variables to the heatmap that only show in the coefficient plot to the heatmap and vice versa

In the below code we’ll add “Visit V12” to the coefficient plot as an example

If you exclude a variable it will not show in the subsequent plot
If you include a variable that does not exist the replot function will ignore the variables you provide

For example, if you try to include “Visit V1” it will not work as it is the reference value


Set the order of variables for replotting.

#Set order of Coefficient plot variables
coef_plot_variables <- c("Sample Cheek", "Sample Scalp")
#Set order of heatmap variables for plot recreation
#Do not include V1 since it is the reference
heatmap_variables <-
    c("Visit V2","Visit V3","Visit V4","Visit V5","Visit V6",
     "Visit V7","Visit V8","Visit V9","Visit V10","Visit V11","Visit V12")

Replot with the coefficient and heatmap variables.
Note: You can include the parameters coef_plot_vars and heatmap_vars in the maaslin3() function. However, it is normally best to see the first summary plot maaslin3() creates and then replot with the parameters so you can see what you want to change.

#MaAsLin3 replot
maaslin3::maaslin_plot_results_from_output(
    output = 'PMP_output',
    metadata = metadata,
    normalization = 'NONE',
    transform = 'LOG',
    max_significance = 0.1,
    median_comparison_abundance = FALSE,
    median_comparison_prevalence = FALSE,
    #Coefficient and heatmap variables
    coef_plot_vars = coef_plot_variables,
    heatmap_vars = heatmap_variables,
    max_pngs = 500
)

You’ll then get the below summary plot.




In these features we can see there is not a great deal of significant associations for the visit comparisons. However, the comparisons with the most associations are Cheek and Scalp. Therefore, it is possible that there’s more interesting results for visit that we’ll need to find with the significant results table and individual figures. Let’s investigate with some tasks and MCQs after looking at the significant results table.

title: Categorical linear boxplots
text:
Boxplots are used to visualise relative/linear modelling associations with categorical fixed effects. To learn about these we will look at Sample site association to interpret the metrics and plots. As a reminder the Sample site comparisons were:

Cheek (query) against Axilla (reference)
Scalp (query) against Axilla (reference)

In this page we will:

Create a significant results table with only abundance model and SampleSite rows
Explain the coefficient value
View and display box plots






To start we’ll create a significant result tibble only containing SampleSite associations with the abundance model.

#Tibble of significant abundance associations for SampleSite
sig_res_samplesite_abund_tbl <- sig_res_tbl |> 
    #Filter to retain metadata SampleSite rows
    dplyr::filter(metadata == "SampleSite") |>
    #Filter to retain abundance model rows
    dplyr::filter(model == "abundance")
#Glimpse the tibble
sig_res_samplesite_abund_tbl |> dplyr::glimpse()

With this tibble we’ll extract the top 5 associations by their q-values (qval_individual).

#Top 5 lowest qval individual values species by abundance for SampleSite
sig_res_samplesite_abund_tbl |> 
    #Extract the five rows with the smallest qval_individual values
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5) |>
    #Select rows of interest
    dplyr::select(c(1:5,9,12))

You’ll see the top association is:

feature: JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290
name: SampleSiteScalp
coefficient: 5.639159

But what does the coefficient mean?






The coefficient value of abundance modelling with categorical data represents the fold change in relative abundance from the reference group to the query group. MaAsLin manual’s description is: “a one-unit change in the metadatum variable corresponds to a 2coef fold change in the relative abundance of the feature.”
In our data the coefficient of JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290 for Scalp is 5.639159. This indicates the relative abundance of the feature is >32 times larger in scalp (query) samples compared to axilla (reference) samples.
In the above we say >32 times as 25 is 32. The actual value of 2^5.639159 is 49.83747.
Below is a table of 2 to the power of:

1

1024



If the value is negative it indicates the relative abundance of the feature is 2coef times less in the query compared to the reference.






With an understanding of the coefficient value we’ll now look at the boxplot for the SampleSite association of JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290.
You can open it in the jupyter file explorer. Its path is ./metagenome_output/figures/association_plots/SampleSite/linear/SampleSite_JANJXX01.sp..hMGS.05290_linear.png. The breakdown of this path is:

./: Reprsents the work directory of your jupyter notebook
metagenome_output/: The output directory specified and created by maaslin3()
figures/: The directory with all output figures
association_plots/: Contains directories of association figures

Each subdirectory is for each specified fixed effect (i.e. Reads, SampleSite, and TimepointName)

SampleSite: Directory for SampleSite association figures containing 2 subdirectories

linear: Contains linear/relative model figures
logistic: Contains logistic/prevalence model figures


Note: As a reminder plots are only created for significant associations.
If the figure is particularly interesting you can display it in your jupyter notebook.

#Plot for JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290
IRdisplay::display_png(file=
    "./metagenome_output/figures/association_plots/SampleSite/linear/SampleSite_JANJXX01.sp..hMGS.05290_linear.png",
    width = 600)

For convenience the plot is shown below:




The features of the plot are:

x-axis: The different categorical values of the fixed effect (Axilla, Cheek, and Scalp).

Shows the number of samples for each category (e.g. Axilla has 34 samples)

y-axis: The abundance of the feature (JANJXX01 sp. hMGS.05290)

These values are the log base 2 of the relative abundance values (total sum scaling)

Top right: Statistical information

Log scale to relative abundance:

-6.908



With this plot we can clearly see that the relative abundance of the scalp samples is much higher than for Axilla. The median value for Scalp is around -5 (relative abundance = ~0.005) whereas its around -12 (relative abundance = ~ 0.00001) for Axilla.
It also looks like Cheek vs Axilla might be a significant associations. Open your significant_results.html and search for “JANJXX01”. You’ll see that the association for Cheek is present with a coefficient of 4.05 and a q-value of 2.34e-7.






Please attempt the following tasks and MCQs:


View the metrics of the top five significant SampleSite associations with negative coefficient values. I.e. filter out associations with positive values and then look at the five associations with the lowest q values

Which feature association has the highest significance (i.e lowest q-value)?

 Staphylococcus epidermidis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteCheek Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus hominis-SampleSiteScalp Cutibacterium avidum-SampleSite Cheek



Which feature association has the greatest coefficient value (i.e. the value furtherest from 0 representing the largest fold change difference comparing the query to the reference)?

 Staphylococcus epidermidis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteCheek Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus hominis-SampleSiteScalp Cutibacterium avidum-SampleSite Cheek



Which feature association has the highest prevalence (i.e. the largest N_not_zero value)?

 Staphylococcus epidermidis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteCheek Staphylococcus lugdunensis-SampleSiteScalp Staphylococcus hominis-SampleSiteScalp Cutibacterium avidum-SampleSite Cheek




Code solution


#View the metrics of the top five significant associations with negative coefficients
sig_res_samplesite_abund_tbl |> 
    #Retain rows with a coefficient value less than 0
    dplyr::filter(coef < 0) |>
    dplyr::slice_min(qval_individual, n=5)





Using the relevant plots answer the below MCQs:

Approximately, what is the median log relative abundance of Staphylococcus epidermidis for the Axilla samples?

 -2.5 -12.5 -15.0



Approximately, what is the median log relative abundance of Staphylococcus hominis for the Scalp samples?

 -2.5 -12.5 -15.0



Approximately, what is the median log relative abundance of Staphylococcus lugdunensis for the Scalp samples?

 -2.5 -12.5 -15.0









Abundance association tests only use samples where the feature is present. This means associations where the feature is more prevalent will have greater power.
This can be seen with our Staphylococcus lugdunensis associations. The fold change is greater for the Scalp against Axilla association (-8.56) compared to the Cheek against Axilla association (-5.14). However, the q-value of the Cheek association is more significant (6e-11 compared to 1.6-e09). This is because there are 26 Cheek samples with S.lugdunensis present compared to only 11 for the Scalp samples.

With this plot we can clearly see that the relative abundance of the scalp samples is much higher than for Axilla. The median value for Scalp is around -5 (relative abundance = ~0.005) whereas its around -12 (relative abundance = ~ 0.00001) for Axilla.
It also looks like Cheek vs Axilla might be a significant associations. Open your significant_results.html and search for “JANJXX01”. You’ll see that the association for Cheek is present with a coefficient of 4.05 and a q-value of 2.34e-7.
